For my college capstone, I picked PHP as the dev language. But the environment wasn't mature then — PHP was transitioning from 4.x to 5.x, no frameworks were available, and the entire dev process was DIY-grinding. Brutal.
"Ray, does your PHP have security defenses in place?" the teacher asked during my capstone build.
Honestly, I hadn't thought about it. Since the teacher specifically emphasized it, clearly it was important. So I started wondering: what exactly needs attention?
While I was still thinking, the teacher's next sentence cut my thinking off —

"When you present, you'll first give the project to the department head and other teachers for trial use. But remember not to give them the admin panel — operate it yourselves during the presentation. That'll prevent other teachers from messing with it." he warned.
Wait... so there's actually a reason behind that warning.

Capstones aren't just a student competition — teachers compete with each other too. Whose student produces the most impressive output? So sometimes there are little schemes, little competitions behind the scenes.

I could only think to myself:
"Whoa, the capstone battlefield isn't just student-vs-student. There's a teacher drama backstage too, XD."

After a chaotic journey to the capstone presentation, with the teacher's strict attention to detail — from slides to delivery — they really pulled us up. The presentation day went smoothly. Got praise from department teachers and the project was even picked as recruiting material.
For my group, it was a clean turnaround victory.
The presentation looked glamorous, but when I later studied security defense and went back to look at our system — it wasn't a project, it was a beehive, full of holes XD.
As for our capstone system… I broke it myself eventually. The ending was more spectacular than the presentation. XD
What's security defense? In short: don't get hacked, or prevent damage after being hacked.
Some people may not know what "hacker" means. Hackers come in three categories:
exist to protect systems. They find vulnerabilities and notify admins to patch — also called ethical hackers.
prioritize their own benefit. Exploit vulnerabilities for illegal gain.
The ones we least want to deal with are black-hats.
— caused massive losses to businesses and individuals.

In earlier chapters we mentioned viruses are a pain. Some can't be solved by reinstalling the OS, because they may have embedded in your files or backups — so even after reinstall, you get re-infected.
Quick word on WannaCry — the dreaded virus of its time.
is ransomware that exploited a Microsoft Windows vulnerability. Once infected, it encrypts your files and demands Bitcoin to unlock.

— characterized by a pair of keys: public and private. The public key encrypts data; only the private key decrypts. So if you don't pay the hacker's ransom, you can't get the private key — your files are permanently locked.
For more on encryption, see my earlier post:
What's encryption? Why encrypt? Basic concepts of encryption
You might think: "Just back up regularly?" Right — that's one approach. But what if the backups are also infected? Restoring brings the virus back.

Honestly, I got infected once early on. I'd backed up to cloud storage but had no rollback feature, so the backup got infected too. Luckily the data wasn't important, so I just gave up. Bitcoin price at the time meant the ransom would've been tens of thousands of TWD.
That's why people emphasize: when major security vulnerabilities appear, update immediately. Not updating is like leaving the front door wide open for hackers.
copy important data to other storage devices — external drive, USB, etc.
back up to other locations — cloud storage, remote server, etc.
Each has pros and cons. Local is faster and cheaper, but if your computer is compromised, local backups can be infected too. Off-site avoids that but is slower and more expensive.
We've been talking personal-computer security. What about when we're writing code? Different angle.
— exploits programs that don't properly filter or escape user input in SQL statements, letting attackers inject malicious SQL to manipulate the database.
Back when ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) wasn't yet popular, people wrote SQL directly to operate the DB, so SQL injection was particularly common.
Example PHP from the previous chapter:
language-php shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Successfully connected to MySQL!"; } else { echo "
Failed to connect to MySQL!
"; } mysqli_close($connection); ?>
Typically users enter credentials on screen, the program sends to the DB to verify — using SQL like:
$sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '$username' AND password = '$password'";
. A match means credentials are correct.
language-sql shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' OR '1'='1' AND password = '$password'
This means "select users whose username is 'admin' OR '1' = '1'". Since '1' = '1' is always true, this returns ALL users — bypassing auth and logging in directly.
Great question. In SQL, OR has higher precedence than AND. So this SQL evaluates the OR part first, then the AND part. End result: all users returned.
Last, another common attack — when building DBs, we set type constraints on columns (e.g., username and password are strings).
But if no length constraint is set, attackers can exploit this.
What's the attack? It's a form of DoS (Denial of Service). Drop a super-long string into a column, the DB consumes massive resources processing it, eventually crashing the system.
When WannaCry hit, friends around me got infected one by one — and I wasn't spared. Luckily my data wasn't important, so I just gave up.
Anyone else have a WannaCry experience?

The capstone presentation looked smooth, but a second look revealed a hive full of holes — from teacher's heads-up to actual landmines: WannaCry, backup strategies, SQL injection, and column-length DoS lessons learned the hard way.
